overview: The Gemara investigates whether identifying marks are a biblical or rabbinic requirement, analyzing various proofs and Rava's conclusion that they are biblical based on the verse 'ad derosh achicha oto'. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara investigates whether identifying marks are a biblical or rabbinic requirement, analyzing various proofs and Rava's conclusion that they are biblical based on the verse 'ad derosh achicha oto'. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Practical difference: Returning a lost גט
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains the practical difference: if סימנים are biblical, a lost גט can be returned based on marks; if rabbinic, it cannot, as rabbis do not enact decrees regarding prohibitions.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא answers its own question about the practical difference. It focuses on the case of returning a גט.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The distinction hinges on the principle that rabbinic enactments apply to monetary matters but not to prohibitions. A גט involves the prohibition of adultery, so if סימנים are only rabbinic, they cannot override the biblical prohibition.

keyTerms.meaning: To return a woman's bill of divorce

steps.whatToRemember: The practical difference is about returning a גט based on סימנים.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets the stakes for the entire sugya.

phrases.english: with regard to returning the bill of divorce of a woman that was lost by an agent before its delivery, on the basis of distinguishing marks.
If you say that the identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is by Torah law, we return the document
But if you say that it is by rabbinic law,
because when the Sages institute an ordinance, it is only with regard to monetary matters
but with regard to ritual matters,
the Sages do not institute an ordinance.

steps.triggerLanguage: לְאַהְדּוֹרֵי גֵט אִשָּׁה בְּסִימָנִים.

rashi.hebrew: לאהדורי גט - שאבד מן השליח המביאו קודם שנתן לה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the case of returning a גט refers to one that was lost by the שליח before he delivered it to the אשה.

rashi.hebrew: בממונא - דהפקר ב"ד הפקר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the principle of תקנתא in ממון is based on the authority of הפקר ב

steps.title: Proof from the Mishnah about garments
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites a Mishnah that includes garments in the category of items requiring proclamation, noting that garments have distinguishing marks, implying סימנים are biblical.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings a proof from a משנה about returning lost items. It highlights the case of garments.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The משנה singles out garments because they have סימנים. The גמרא infers that since the תורה requires proclamation for items with סימנים, סימנים must be a biblical requirement.
steps.whatToRemember: The proof is from the משנה's inclusion of garments.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is the first attempt to prove סימנים are biblical.

phrases.aramaic: תָּא שְׁמַע: אַף הַשִּׂמְלָה הָיְתָה בִּכְלַל כׇּל אֵלּוּ,
phrases.english: The Gemara suggests: Come and hear proof from the mishna: The garment was also included in the generalization that one must return all of these items.

phrases.aramaic: וְלָמָּה יָצָאת לְהַקִּישׁ אֵלֶיהָ? וְלוֹמַר לְךָ:
phrases.english: And why did it emerge from the generalization that is should be specified? To draw an analogy to it and to say to you:

phrases.aramaic: מָה שִׂמְלָה מְיוּחֶדֶת שֶׁיֵּשׁ לָהּ סִימָנִין
phrases.english: What is notable about a garment? It is notable in that there are distinguishing marks concerning it
and it has claimants asserting ownership, and its finder is obligated to proclaim his find.

phrases.aramaic: אַף כׇּל דָּבָר שֶׁיֵּשׁ לוֹ סִימָנִין
phrases.english: So too with regard to any item concerning which there are distinguishing marks
and it has claimants asserting ownership, its finder is obligated to proclaim his find.

phrases.aramaic: תַּנָּא: תּוֹבְעִין אִצְטְרִיכָא לֵיהּ, סִימָנִין כְּדִי נַסְבַהּ.
phrases.english: The Gemara rejects the proof: Perhaps it was necessary for the tanna to mention only the criterion of claimants, and the tanna cited the criterion of distinguishing marks for no reason, as by Torah law distinguishing marks are not a relevant factor.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּא שְׁמַע: אַף הַשִּׂמְלָה הָיְתָה בִּכְלַל כׇּל אֵלּוּ

rashi.hebrew: תובעין אצטריכא ליה - קרא לתובעין אתא ולמעוטי מידי דאיאוש ואפקריה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishnah's mention of תובעין is necessary to exclude cases where the owner has already יאוש and אפקריה, meaning the biblical requirement for proclamation only applies when there are active claimants.

rashi.hebrew: כדי נסבה - בלא מקרא אלא אסמכינהו אקרא בעלמא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the requirement for סימנים is not derived from a specific biblical verse but is merely אסמכינהו אקרא בעלמא, meaning it is a rabbinic enactment based on the general biblical framework.

steps.title: Proof from returning a donkey based on saddle marks
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites a rule that a donkey is returned based on the marks of its saddle, implying סימנים are biblical.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings another proof from a different source. It focuses on the case of a donkey and saddle.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The rule about the donkey and saddle is presented as a clear case of returning based on סימנים. The גמרא infers that this must be biblical law.
steps.whatToRemember: The proof is from the case of a donkey and its saddle.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is a second attempt to prove סימנים are biblical.

phrases.english: Come and hear proof from the aforementioned statement: The obligation to return a donkey to its owner on the basis of the distinguishing marks of the saddle is derived based on the mention of the word “donkey” in the verse from Deuteronomy. Clearly, the identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is by Torah law.

Emend the baraita and say: There is an obligation to return the donkey only on the basis of witnesses who testify with regard to the identity of the owner based on the fact that the saddle belongs to him, and not on the basis of distinguishing marks.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּא שְׁמַע: חֲמוֹר בְּסִימָנֵי אוּכָף.

rashi.hebrew: ת"ש - דאוקמינן לעיל חמור לסימני אוכף אתא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the תא שמע here refers back to the earlier discussion where the case of the donkey was established as being based on סימני אוכף.

rashi.hebrew: אימא בעדי אוכף - אם יש עדים המכירין בטביעות עין לאוכף שהוא שלו מחזירין לו את החמור:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara's suggestion to say 'בעדי אוכף' means that if there are witnesses who recognize the specific marks of the saddle as belonging to the claimant, the donkey is returned to him.

tosafot.hebrew: ת"ש חמור בסימני אוכף - ברייתא היא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות notes that the source for the rule about the donkey and saddle marks is a ברייתא.

steps.title: Proof from the verse 'until your brother claims it'
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites the verse 'until your brother claims it' (עד דרש אחיך אתו), arguing that the finder must scrutinize the claimant via סימנים before returning the item.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings a proof from a biblical verse. It interprets the verse as requiring scrutiny of the claimant.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The verse is interpreted to mean that the finder must scrutinize the claimant to determine if he is a swindler. The גמרא infers that this scrutiny involves checking סימנים, implying סימנים are biblical.
steps.whatToRemember: The proof is from the verse about claiming a lost item.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is a direct biblical proof.

phrases.aramaic: תָּא שְׁמַע: ״וְהָיָה עִמְּךָ עַד דְּרֹשׁ אָחִיךָ אֹתוֹ״.
phrases.english: Come and hear proof from a mishna (28b): “And if your brother be not near you, and you know him not, then you shall bring it into your house, and it shall be with you until your brother claims [derosh] it, and you shall return it to him” (Deuteronomy 22:2).

phrases.aramaic: וְכִי תַּעֲלֶה עַל דַּעְתְּךָ שֶׁיִּתְּנֶנּוּ לוֹ קוֹדֶם שֶׁיִּדְרְשֶׁנּוּ?
phrases.english: Would it enter your mind that he would give the lost item to him before he claims it?
How can the finder return it if he does not know the identity of the owner?

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא דׇּרְשֵׁהוּ אִם רַמַּאי הוּא אוֹ אֵינוֹ רַמַּאי.
phrases.english: Scrutinize him [darshehu] to determine whether the claimant is a swindler or whether he is not a swindler.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּא שְׁמַע: ״וְהָיָה עִמְּךָ עַד דְּרֹשׁ אָחִיךָ אֹתוֹ״.

rashi.hebrew: דרשהו - והכי משמע עד דרוש אחיך אותו עד דרשך את אחיך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the verse 'until your brother claims it' is interpreted to mean 'until you scrutinize your brother' to determine if he is a swindler.

tosafot.hebrew: דרשהו אם הוא רמאי מאי לאו בסימנין - וא"ת דבמתני' (לקמן בבא מציעא כח:) דריש מהאי קרא דלא יחזיר אף בסימנין וי"ל דאסמכתא היא כדקתני עלה בברייתא משרבו הרמאים התקינו אבל הכא משמע ליה שהיא דרשה גמורה מדקאמר וכי תעלה על דעתך לומר כו' ועי"ל דבמתני' מיירי בידוע שהוא רמאי לכך צריך עדים אבל הכא מיירי בסתם בני אדם:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the difficulty that the same verse is used in a later מתינית to derive that one should not return an item even with סימנין, explaining that the later derivation is merely an אסמכתא for a rabbinic enactment, whereas here the Gemara understands it as a full דרשה גמורה for a biblical requirement, or alternatively distinguishing between a known swindler requiring witnesses and a general case.

steps.title: Rejection: Perhaps it means witnesses
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara rejects the proof by suggesting the verse refers to witnesses, not סימנים.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא dismisses the proof by reinterpreting the verse. It suggests the scrutiny involves witnesses.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The rejection is based on the idea that the scrutiny involves witnesses who testify to the claimant's identity, not סימנים. This avoids the implication that סימנים are biblical.
steps.whatToRemember: The proof is rejected by reinterpreting the verse as referring to witnesses.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shows the difficulty of proving biblical status from ambiguous verses.

phrases.english: What, is it not that the one who claims the lost item proves that he is not a swindler on the basis of distinguishing marks that he provides? No, the determination of whether he is a swindler is on the basis of scrutinizing his witnesses.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַאי לָאו בְּסִימָנִין? לֹא, בְּעֵדִים.

rashi.hebrew: בעדים - שיבא עדים שהוא שלו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's rejection of סימנים in favor of עדים means the verse refers to witnesses who testify that the item belongs to the claimant.

steps.title: Proof from testimony about a corpse's face
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites a Mishnah that testimony about a corpse's identity requires seeing the face and nose, not just סימנים on the body or clothes, implying סימנים are not biblical.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings a proof from a משנה about identifying a corpse. It focuses on the requirement to see the face and nose.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The משנה states that testimony about a corpse's identity requires seeing the face and nose, even if there are סימנים on the body or clothes. The גמרא infers that סימנים are not sufficient for biblical identification, implying they are only rabbinic.
steps.whatToRemember: The proof is from the משנה about identifying a corpse.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is a proof that סימנים are not biblical.

phrases.english: Come and hear:

phrases.aramaic: אֵין מְעִידִין אֶלָּא עַל פַּרְצוּף הַפָּנִים עִם הַחוֹטֶם,
phrases.english: One testifies only about the countenance of the face with the nose,

phrases.aramaic: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁיֵּשׁ סִימָנִין בְּגוּפוֹ וּבְכֵלָיו.
phrases.english: even though there are distinguishing marks on his body and on his garments.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּא שְׁמַע: אֵין מְעִידִין אֶלָּא עַל פַּרְצוּף הַפָּנִים עִם הַחוֹטֶם

rashi.hebrew: אין מעידין - על אדם שמת להשיא את אשתו אלא אם כן ראו פרצוף פניו שהוא עם החוטם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishnah's rule about identifying a corpse by the face and nose applies specifically to permitting a widow to remarry.

steps.title: Resolution: The marks mentioned are non-specific or subject to loan
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the proof by explaining that the marks on the body are non-specific (tall/short) and the marks on clothes are subject to the concern of loan.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא answers the proof by distinguishing the types of marks. It argues the marks are either non-specific or subject to loan.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution is that the marks on the body are non-specific and the marks on clothes are subject to the concern of loan. This avoids the implication that סימנים are not biblical.
steps.whatToRemember: The proof is resolved by distinguishing the types of marks.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shows how the גמרא defends the biblical status of סימנים.

phrases.english: Conclude from it that the identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is not by Torah law.
The distinguishing marks on his body mentioned in the mishna are non-specific distinguishing marks, e.g., that he was tall or short
The distinguishing marks on his garments mentioned in the mishna are ineffective in determining his identity, as we are concerned about the possibility of a loan

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמְרִי: גּוּפוֹ – דְּאָרוֹךְ וְגוּץ. כֵּלָיו – דְּחָיְישִׁינַן לִשְׁאֵלָה.

rashi.hebrew: דארוך או גוץ - שאין זה סימן שהרבה ארוכין וגוצים יש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the marks on the body (גופו) are non-specific because many people are tall or short, so they do not constitute valid biblical identification.

rashi.hebrew: חיישינן - שמא השאילם לאחר ואותו אחר ראו שמת:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern regarding the clothes (כליו) is that the owner may have lent them to someone else who then saw the death, meaning the claimant might be the borrower rather than the owner.

steps.title: Challenge: How do we return a donkey based on saddle marks if we worry about loans?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the resolution by asking how we can return a donkey based on saddle marks if we are concerned about loans.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against the previous resolution. It questions the case of the donkey and saddle.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The challenge is based on the earlier case of the donkey and saddle. If we are concerned about loans, how can we return the donkey based on the saddle marks? This creates a contradiction.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is about the donkey and saddle case.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shows the difficulty of maintaining the resolution.

phrases.aramaic: אִי חָיְישִׁינַן לִשְׁאֵלָה, חֲמוֹר בְּסִימָנֵי אוּכָּף הֵיכִי מַהְדְּרִינַן?
phrases.english: If we are concerned about the possibility of a loan, how do we return a donkey to its owner on the basis of the distinguishing marks of the saddle; perhaps it was borrowed?

phrases.aramaic: אָמְרִי: אוּכָּף, לָא שָׁאיְלִי אִינָשֵׁי אוּכָּפָא,
phrases.english: The Sages say in response: People do not typically borrow a saddle
because saddles that are not custom fit wound the donkey.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִי חָיְישִׁינַן לִשְׁאֵלָה, חֲמוֹר בְּסִימָנֵי אוּכָף הֵיכִי מַהְדְּרִינַן?

rashi.hebrew: מסקב - דורוייש"ר בלעז:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that מסקב means the saddle becomes loose or falls off, which is why people do not borrow saddles.

steps.title: Alternative resolution: The marks on clothes are non-specific
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara offers an alternative resolution: the marks on the clothes are non-specific (white/red).
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא offers an alternative תירוץ to the earlier challenge. It suggests the marks on the clothes were non-specific colors.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution is that the marks were simply white and red (בְּחִיוּרֵי וּבְסוּמְקִי). These are common colors, not unique identifiers, so there is no concern of a loan.

phrases.english: If you wish, say instead: The distinguishing marks on his garments mentioned in the mishna are non-specific distinguishing marks, e.g., where the witness said that they were white or red, and that is the reason that the distinguishing marks are ineffective in determining his identity.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיבָּעֵית אֵימָא: כֵּלָיו בְּחִיוּרֵי וּבְסוּמְקִי.

rashi.hebrew: חיורי או סומקי - אין זה סימן שהרבה כאלה יש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that white or red clothing are not valid identifying marks because such colors are common and therefore non-specific.

steps.title: Challenge: How do we return a גט based on marks if we worry about loans?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the resolution by asking how we can return a גט based on marks if we are concerned about loans.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a קושיא from a ברייתא about returning a גט. It questions how marks can validate a גט if loans are a concern.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא discusses returning a גט found in a pouch, purse, or ring. If marks are only rabbinic, the finder might claim it was a loan, undermining the return.

phrases.english: But there is that which is taught in a baraita:

phrases.aramaic: מְצָאוֹ קָשׁוּר בְּכִיס אוֹ בְּאַרְנָקִי וּבְטַבַּעַת,
phrases.english: If the agent found the bill of divorce that he lost bound to his pouch, or his purse, or his signet ring,
or if he found it among his garments,
even if he found it a long time after he lost it, the distinguishing marks on those items are sufficient in order to identify the bill of divorce as the one that he lost, and it is valid.
And if it enters your mind that we are concerned about the possibility of a loan,

phrases.aramaic: כִּי מְצָאוֹ קָשׁוּר בְּכִיס אַמַּאי כָּשֵׁר? נֵיחוּשׁ לִשְׁאֵלָה!
phrases.english: when he found the bill of divorce bound to his pouch, why is it valid? Let us be concerned about the possibility of a loan and that perhaps the pouch and the bill of divorce belong to someone else.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא הָא דְּתַנְיָא: מְצָאוֹ קָשׁוּר בְּכִיס אוֹ בְּאַרְנָקִי וּבְטַבַּעַת

rashi.hebrew: מצאו קשור בכיס שלו או בארנקי כו' - שליח המביא גט ואבד הימנו לאחר זמן מצאו קשור בכיס שלו או בארנקי כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the baraita discusses a case where a messenger lost a גט and later found it tied in his own כיס or ארנקי.

rashi.hebrew: בין כליו - כלי תשמישו שבביתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that בין כליו refers to the messenger's personal utensils found in his home.

rashi.hebrew: אפילו לזמן מרובה - אע"ג דתנן (גיטין דף כז.) המביא גט ואבד הימנו מצאו לזמן מרובה פסול הכא כשר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that although the Mishnah in גיטין rules a גט found after a long time is פסול, this baraita rules it כשר when found in the messenger's personal belongings.

rashi.hebrew: ניחוש לשאלה - שמא השאיל כיסו לאחר והוא קשרו בו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern of ניחוש לשאלה is that the messenger might have lent his כיס to someone else, who then tied the גט in it.

tosafot.hebrew: מצאו - גט קשור בכיס יחזיר המוצא לשליח או לבעל השואלו בסימני הכיס:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the finder returns the גט to the messenger or the borrower based on the marks of the כיס.

tosafot.hebrew: (וניחוש לשאלה) - וא"ת ולוכח מהכא דסימנין דאורייתא ובפרק בתרא דיבמות (דף קכ. ושם ד"ה מצאו) פריך מהכא דסימנין דאורייתא וי"ל דמצי לאוקמה כגון שאותו שאבדו מצאו ומכיר הכיס בטביעות עין דכיון שבעצמו מצאו נאמן וה"נ הוה מצי למדחי התם:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses a potential proof that סימנים are דאורייתא, suggesting the case could be where the messenger himself found the גט and recognized the כיס by sight, making him נאמן.

steps.title: Resolution: People do not loan pouches, purses, or rings
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by explaining that people do not loan pouches, purses, or rings due to concerns about loss of fortune or forgery.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא answers the challenge by distinguishing these items. It states people do not loan pouches, purses, or rings.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The reason is fear of bad luck (מְסַמְּנִי) or forgery (מְזַיְּפִי). Since these items are not loaned, the concern of a loan does not apply here.

phrases.aramaic: אָמְרִי: כִּיס וְאַרְנָקִי וְטַבַּעַת לָא מַשְׁאֲלִי אִינָשֵׁי.
phrases.english: The Sages say in response: There is no concern in this case, as people do not loan a pouch, a purse, or a signet ring to another person.
One does not loan his pouch and his purse to others due to the fact that it portends the loss of his good fortune.
And one does not loan his signet ring to others due to the fact that it could be used to forge documents.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמְרִי: כִּיס וְאַרְנָקִי וְטַבַּעַת לָא מַשְׁאֲלִי אִינָשֵׁי.

rashi.hebrew: דמסמני - מנחשי ואומר סימן לאדם שמשאיל כיסו שמוכר לו מזלו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern of מסמני refers to fortune-telling, where a person who lends their pouch allows the borrower to cast a spell or sign over their luck.

rashi.hebrew: דמזייף - שהיה לכל איש חותם ניכר בטבעתו וכששולח שום דבר סתום מכסהו וחותמו בטבעתו לפיכך אינו משאיל טבעתו שמא יעשה זה חותם כנגד חותמו ויזייף בו את שליחותיו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern of מזייף regarding a ring is that since each person has a distinct seal on their ring used to authenticate sealed documents, lending it risks the borrower creating a counterfeit seal to forge the lender's correspondence.

tosafot.hebrew: כיס וארנקי משום דמסמני - וא"ת והכתיב לא תנחשו וי"ל בשלהי במה אשה (שבת דף סז.) אמרינן כל דבר שיש בו משום רפואה אין בו משום דרכי האמורי:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the contradiction with the prohibition of לא תנחשו by citing the principle from שבת that practices intended for healing do not fall under the prohibition of דרכי האמורי.

steps.title: Question: Is the dispute about the status of סימנים?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks if the dispute between the Tanna Kamma and Elazar ben Mahavai about testifying to a mole is about whether סימנים are biblical or rabbinic.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a שאלה about the crux of a dispute regarding a שומא. It asks if the dispute is about whether סימנים are biblical or rabbinic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The dispute involves אֶלְעָזָר בֶּן מַהֲבַאי and others testifying to a mole. The גמרא wonders if their disagreement stems from the status of marks.

phrases.aramaic: לֵימָא כְתַנָּאֵי: אֵין מְעִידִין עַל הַשּׁוּמָא,
phrases.english: The Gemara suggests: Let us say that the dilemma whether the identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is by Torah law or by rabbinic law is the subject of a dispute between tanna’im, as it is taught in a baraita: One does not testify on the basis of a mole on the body of the deceased to determine the identity of a man who died and permit his wife to remarry.
And Elazar ben Mahavai says:
One testifies to identify the corpse on the basis of a mole.
What, is it not with regard to this matter that they disagree;
as the first tanna holds that identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is by rabbinic law

phrases.aramaic: וְאֶלְעָזָר בֶּן מַהֲבַאי סָבַר: סִימָנִין דְּאוֹרָיְיתָא.
phrases.english: and Elazar ben Mahavai holds that identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is by Torah law.

steps.triggerLanguage: לֵימָא כְּתַנָּאֵי: אֵין מְעִידִין עַל הַשּׁוּמָא

rashi.hebrew: אין מעידין על השומא - המעידין על האשה להשיאה ואמרו סימן היה בו שומא באבר פלוני:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishnaic phrase 'אין מעידין על השומא' refers to witnesses testifying about a woman's identifying mark (a mole) to permit her marriage.

rashi.hebrew: סימנין דרבנן - וגבי אשת איש דאיסור דאורייתא הוא לא סמכינן עלייהו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Tanna Kamma holds סימנים are only דרבנן, and therefore cannot be relied upon to override an איסור דאורייתא like the prohibition of אשת איש.

steps.title: Resolution: The dispute is about whether a mole is common in contemporaries
steps.stepSummary: Rava resolves the question by explaining that the dispute is about whether a mole is common in contemporaries, not about the status of סימנים.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא answers the שאלה by redefining the dispute. He states everyone agrees סימנים are biblical.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The actual dispute is whether a mole is common in contemporaries (בֶּן גִּילוֹ). If it is common, it is not a valid mark, regardless of biblical status.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רָבָא: דְּכוּלֵּי עָלְמָא סִימָנִין דְּאוֹרָיְיתָא,
phrases.english: Rava said: That is not necessarily the crux of their dispute, as perhaps everyone agrees that identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is by Torah law,

phrases.aramaic: וְהָכָא בְּשׁוּמָא מְצוּיָה בְּבֶן גִּילוֹ קָמִיפַּלְגִי.
phrases.english: and here, it is with regard to whether one needs to be concerned that a mole is often found on one’s contemporary, i.e., one born under the same constellation, rendering it useless as a means of identification, that they disagree.

phrases.aramaic: מָר סָבַר: שׁוּמָא מְצוּיָה בְּבֶן גִּילוֹ,
phrases.english: One Sage, the first tanna, holds that a mole is often found on one’s contemporary

phrases.aramaic: וּמָר סָבַר: שׁוּמָא אֵינָהּ מְצוּיָה בְּבֶן גִּילוֹ.
phrases.english: and one Sage, Elazar ben Mahavai, holds that a mole is not often found on one’s contemporary.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָבָא: דְּכוּלֵּי עָלְמָא סִימָנִין דְּאוֹרָיְיתָא

rashi.hebrew: בבן גילו - הנולד בשעתו שנולדו במזל אחד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase בן גילו refers to someone born at the same time, specifically under the same astrological sign.

steps.title: Alternative resolution: The dispute is about whether marks change after death
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara offers an alternative resolution: the dispute is about whether marks change after death.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא offers an alternative תירוץ to the שאלה. It suggests the dispute is about marks changing after death.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The question is whether סימנים are liable to change (לְהִשְׁתַּנּוֹת) after death (לְאַחַר מִיתָה). This is a separate issue from the biblical status of marks.

phrases.english: If you wish, say instead that

phrases.aramaic: דְּכוּלֵּי עָלְמָא שׁוּמָא אֵינָהּ מְצוּיָה בְּבֶן גִּילוֹ,
phrases.english: everyone agrees that a mole is not often found on one’s contemporary,

phrases.aramaic: וְהָכָא בְּסִימָנִין הָעֲשׂוּיִן לְהִשְׁתַּנּוֹת לְאַחַר מִיתָה קָמִיפַּלְגִי.
phrases.english: and here it is with regard to whether the appearance of distinguishing marks on the body is apt to change after death that they disagree.

phrases.aramaic: מָר סָבַר: סִימָנִין עֲשׂוּיִם לְהִשְׁתַּנּוֹת לְאַחַר מִיתָה.
phrases.english: One Sage, the first tanna, holds that the appearance of distinguishing marks is apt to change after death,

phrases.aramaic: וּמָר סָבַר: סִימָנִין אֵין עֲשׂוּיִם לְהִשְׁתַּנּוֹת לְאַחַר מִיתָה.
phrases.english: and one Sage, Elazar ben Mahavai, holds that the appearance of distinguishing marks is not apt to change after death.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיבָּעֵית אֵימָא: דְּכוּלֵּי עָלְמָא שׁוּמָא אֵינָהּ מְצוּיָה בְּבֶן גִּילוֹ

rashi.hebrew: בסימנין העשויין כו' - אם עשויין להשתנות או אינן עשויין להשתנות אם בחייו היתה שומא שחורה במותו נעשית לבנה או איפוך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the dispute concerns whether a mark (שומא) that is black during a person's life turns white after death, or vice versa.

steps.title: Alternative resolution: The dispute is about whether a mole is a clear mark
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara offers another alternative resolution: the dispute is about whether a mole is a clear mark.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides a third alternative תירוץ. It suggests the dispute is about whether a mole is a clear mark.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The question is whether a שומא is a סִימָן מוּבְהָק. This is another way to explain the dispute without questioning the biblical status of סימנים.

phrases.english: If you wish, say instead that

phrases.aramaic: דְּכוּלֵּי עָלְמָא שׁוּמָא אֵינָהּ עֲשׂוּיָה לְהִשְׁתַּנּוֹת לְאַחַר מִיתָה,
phrases.english: everyone agrees that a mole is not apt to change after death,

phrases.aramaic: וְסִימָנִין דְּרַבָּנַן, וְהָכָא בְּשׁוּמָא סִימָן מוּבְהָק הוּא קָמִיפַּלְגִי.
phrases.english: and that the identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is by rabbinic law, and here it is with regard to whether a mole is a clear-cut distinguishing mark that they disagree.

phrases.aramaic: מָר סָבַר: שׁוּמָא סִימָן מוּבְהָק הוּא,
phrases.english: One Sage, Elazar ben Mahavai, holds that a mole is a clear-cut distinguishing mark

phrases.aramaic: וּמָר סָבַר: שׁוּמָא לָאו סִימָן מוּבְהָק הוּא.
phrases.english: and one Sage, the first tanna, holds that a mole is not a clear-cut distinguishing mark.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיבָּעֵית אֵימָא: דְּכוּלֵּי עָלְמָא שׁוּמָא אֵינָהּ עֲשׂוּיָה לְהִשְׁתַּנּוֹת לְאַחַר מִיתָה

rashi.hebrew: מובהק - ואפילו סימנין דרבנן יש לסמוך על זה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that when the Gemara calls a mole a סימן מובהק, it means that even though סימנים are only דרבנן, one can rely on this specific mark.

steps.title: Question: How do we return lost items based on marks if they are not biblical?
steps.stepSummary: Rava asks how we can return lost items based on marks if they are not biblical, implying that the finder would not want to return them.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא raises a שאלה about the practical implications of סימנים being rabbinic. He questions how we can enforce the return of אֲבֵידָה.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If סימנים are only rabbinic, the מוֹצֵא אֲבֵידָה might refuse to return the item. This creates a practical problem for the law of lost items.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רָבָא: אִם תִּמְצֵי לוֹמַר סִימָנִין לָאו דְּאוֹרָיְיתָא,
phrases.english: Rava says: If you say that the identification of an item on the basis of distinguishing marks is not by Torah law,
how do we return a lost item to the presumed owner on the basis of distinguishing marks;

phrases.aramaic: דְּנִיחָא לֵיהּ לְמוֹצֵא אֲבֵידָה דְּנַהְדַּר בְּסִימָנִין,
phrases.english: as it is satisfactory to the finder of a lost item to return it on the basis of distinguishing marks,

phrases.aramaic: כִּי הֵיכִי דְּכִי אָבְדָה לֵיהּ לְדִידֵהּ
phrases.english: so that when an item is lost from him in the future,
the finder will return it to him on the basis of distinguishing marks as well.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָבָא: אִם תִּמְצֵא לוֹמַר סִימָנִין לָאו דְּאוֹרָיְיתָא

rashi.hebrew: היכי מהדרינן - כלומר מה תקנה ראו חכמים בדבר ליכנס בספק להחזיר ממון למי שאינו שלו: אלא ל"ג:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבא's question is asking what practical remedy חכמים established to prevent the dilemma of returning money to someone who might not be the rightful owner.

steps.title: Challenge: Can one benefit from property that is not his?
steps.stepSummary: Rav Safra challenges Rava's answer by asking if one can benefit from property that is not his.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against רבא's previous answer. This is a setup line introducing a new objection.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב סָפְרָא argues that the finder cannot benefit from מָמוֹן שֶׁאֵינוֹ שֶׁלּוֹ. This creates a direct contradiction to רבא's logic.

phrases.english: Rav Safra said to Rava:

phrases.aramaic: וְכִי אָדָם עוֹשֶׂה טוֹבָה לְעַצְמוֹ בְּמָמוֹן שֶׁאֵינוֹ שֶׁלּוֹ?
phrases.english: But can a person perform an act that results in benefit for himself with property that is not his?

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב סָפְרָא לְרָבָא: וְכִי אָדָם עוֹשֶׂה טוֹבָה לְעַצְמוֹ בְּמָמוֹן שֶׁאֵינוֹ שֶׁלּוֹ?

rashi.hebrew: וכי אדם עושה - ובניחותא דמוצא מאי איכפת לן אם מי שאבדה ממנו לא ניחא ליה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that Rav Safra's question implies that if the finder has no personal interest in the lost item, we should not care whether the owner is pleased with the finder's actions.

steps.title: Resolution: The owner wants to return it based on marks
steps.stepSummary: Rava resolves the challenge by explaining that the owner wants to return the item based on marks, as he knows he has no witnesses.
steps.whatsHappening: רבא answers the challenge by distinguishing the owner's perspective. This is a resolution step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבא explains that the בַּעַל אֲבֵידָה wants to return the item based on marks. He knows he has no עֵדִים, so he relies on סימנים.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא נִיחָא לֵיהּ לְבַעַל אֲבֵידָה לְמֵיהַב סִימָנִין וּלְמִשְׁקְלֵיהּ.
phrases.english: Rather, we return the lost item, as it is satisfactory to the owner of the lost item to be able to provide a description using distinguishing marks and on that basis take possession of the item.

phrases.aramaic: מִידָּע יָדַע דְּעֵדִים לֵית לֵיהּ, וּמֵימָר אָמַר:
phrases.english: He knows that he has no witnesses to testify to his ownership, and he says:

phrases.aramaic: כּוּלֵּי עָלְמָא לָא יָדְעִי סִימָנִין מוּבְהָקִים דִּידַהּ,
phrases.english: No one else knows the clear-cut distinguishing marks that are on the item.

phrases.aramaic: וַאֲנָא יָהֵיבְנָא סִימָנִין מוּבְהָקִים דִּידַהּ וְשָׁקֵלְנָא לַהּ.
phrases.english: And I will provide a description using the clear-cut distinguishing marks, and based on that information I will take possession of the item.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא נִיחָא לֵיהּ לְבַעַל אֲבֵידָה לְמֵיהַב סִימָנִין וּלְמִשְׁקְלֵיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: אלא - אמר רבא כל אובדי אבידה ניחא להו שתהא דת זו בישראל למיתב סימנין וכל הבא ונותנו יטלנה מאי טעמא מידע ידע דעדים לית ליה פעמים שאין לו עדים עליה ואם יזקיקוהו לעדים לא תבא לעולם לידו ומימר אמר טוב לי שיחזירוה לכל האומר סימניה דלא שכיחא דנימא סימנין דידה אלא אנא דכ"ע לא ידעי כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the owner prefers returning the item based on marks because he knows he lacks witnesses, and he reasons that it is better to return the item to anyone who claims it with marks, since few people know the specific marks of his lost item.

tosafot.hebrew: ואנא יהיבנא סימנים מובהקין דידה - לא מובהקין ממש קאמר אלא מובהקים מסימני חבירו כמו זה אומר מדת ארכו וזה אומר מדת רחבו ולקמן (בבא מציעא דף כח:) נמי דקאמר לעולם גלימא מכריז ולא אמר סימנין מובהקין התם נמי לאו מובהקים ממש קאמר אלא שלא אמר סימנים טובים שיש בו כי אם כסימנין דסומקי וחיורי:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that the term מובהקין here does not mean definitively unique marks, but rather marks that are more specific than those of another claimant, such as one claiming length and another claiming width.

steps.title: Challenge: How do we return promissory notes based on marks?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the resolution by asking how we can return promissory notes based on marks if the owner wants to return them.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against the previous resolution. This is a setup line introducing a new objection.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The challenge is based on a משנה about returning שְׁטָרוֹת. If the owner wants to return the item based on marks, how can we return the notes to the מַלְוֶה?

phrases.english: But there is that which we learned in a mishna
that Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel says:
If one found three promissory notes relating to the loan of one debtor who borrowed money from three creditors, he must return the documents to the debtor.

phrases.aramaic: שְׁלֹשָׁה שֶׁלָּווּ מִן הָאֶחָד – יַחְזִיר לַמַּלְוֶה.
phrases.english: If one found three promissory notes relating to the loans of three debtors who borrowed money from one creditor, he must return the documents to the creditor.

phrases.aramaic: נִיחָא לֵיהּ לְלֹוֶה לְאַהְדּוֹרֵי לֵיהּ לְמַלְוֶה?
phrases.english: is it satisfactory to the debtor to have the documents returned to the creditor, as doing so would enable the creditor to collect payment of the loan?

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא הָא דִּתְנַן, רַבָּן שִׁמְעוֹן בֶּן גַּמְלִיאֵל אוֹמֵר: אֶחָד הַלּוֶה מִשְּׁלֹשָׁה

rashi.hebrew: אלא הא דתנן כו' - ואמרו להחזיר לו למלוה בלא עדים בסימן זה שאומר שלשה היו ומשלשה לוים ואם מן הלוה נפלו שכבר פרעם מי ניחא ליה בתקנה זו שיתנם המוצאם לכל הבא ונותן סימניהם והלא טוב לו שיהו מונחים ביד המוצאם עולמית דכל זמן שהם בידו ולא יחזירם למלוה הרי הם כשרופים ולא ניחא דליהדר להו בסימנין דלמא מיתרמי דידע מלוה בסימניהן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's challenge is that the borrower would prefer the promissory notes remain with the finder permanently, as long as they are in the finder's possession they are considered as if burned, rather than being returned to the creditor based on marks which might allow the creditor to identify them and collect.

steps.title: Resolution: The return is based on logic, not marks
steps.stepSummary: Rava resolves the challenge by explaining that the return of promissory notes is based on logic, not marks.
steps.whatsHappening: רבא answers the challenge by distinguishing the case of the promissory notes. This is a resolution step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבא explains that the return of שְׁטָרוֹת is based on סְבָרָא, not marks. The notes are returned to the person who typically holds them.

phrases.english: Rava said to Rav Safra: There, it is logical reasoning,
If one found three promissory notes relating to the loan of one debtor who borrowed money from three creditors, he shall return the documents to the debtor –
because a group of several documents indicating that one debtor borrowed money from several creditors is typically found with the debtor,
and is not typically found with a creditor.
Conclude from it that the group of documents fell from the debtor while they were in his possession.
If one found three promissory notes relating to the loans of three debtors who borrowed money from one creditor, he shall return the documents to the creditor –
because a group of several documents indicating that multiple debtors borrowed money from a single creditor is typically found with the creditor,
and is not typically found with a debtor,

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: הָתָם סְבָרָא הוּא

rashi.hebrew: התם - בסימנין כי האי לא איכפת לן בניחותא דלוה דודאי לאו מיניה נפיל דהנך שטרי אחריני מאי בעו גביה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in the case of promissory notes, we do not worry about the borrower's marks because it is clear the notes did not fall from him, as he would have no use for other people's documents.
